content.problemStatement:
You are given a tree with NNN vertices, numbered from 111 to NNN, and a designated root vertex 111.

Each vertex iii has an associated integer value ViV_iVi​.
You must construct a sequence of **values**, B1,B2,…,BNB_1, B_2, \dots, B_NB1​,B2​,…,BN​, according to the following process on the tree:



Initially, only the root vertex is chosen.



Let the set of chosen vertices be SSS.
Initially, S={1}S = \{1\}S={1}.
The sequence of values starts as B1=V1B_1 = V_1B1​=V1​.



At every step, you may choose a vertex xxx such that:



x∉Sx \notin Sx∈/S, and
there exists at least one vertex y∈Sy \in Sy∈S such that there is an edge between xxx and yyy in the tree.

After choosing xxx:



add xxx to the set SSS,
append its value VxV_xVx​ to the sequence BBB.



You must continue this process until all NNN vertices are chosen (so ∣S∣=N|S| = N∣S∣=N and the sequence BBB has length NNN).



Among all possible valid sequences BBB that can be obtained by following these rules, you must output the **lexicographically smallest** one.

We say that AAA is **lexicographically smaller** than BBB if one of the following holds:


There exists an index i≤min⁡(∣A∣,∣B∣)i \le \min(|A|, |B|)i≤min(∣A∣,∣B∣) such that

Aj=BjA_j = B_jAj​=Bj​ for all 1≤j<i1 \le j < i1≤j<i, and
Ai<BiA_i < B_iAi​<Bi​.


Or, AAA is a **proper prefix** of BBB; that is, ∣A∣<∣B∣|A| < |B|∣A∣<∣B∣ and

Aj=BjA_j = B_jAj​=Bj​ for all 1≤j≤∣A∣1 \le j \le |A|1≤j≤∣A∣.

Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, the number of test cases.
For each test case:

The first line contains single integer NNN — the number of vertices in the tree.
The second line contains NNN integers V1,V2,…,VNV_1, V_2, \dots, V_NV1​,V2​,…,VN​, where ViV_iVi​ is the value associated with vertex iii.
The next N−1N - 1N−1 lines each contain two integers uuu and vvv, denoting an undirected edge between vertices uuu and vvv.



It is guaranteed that the given graph is a tree (connected and with exactly N−1N-1N−1 edges).



Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing NNN space-separated integers: the lexicographically smallest valid sequence of values B1,B2,…,BNB_1, B_2, \dots, B_NB1​,B2​,…,BN​ that can be constructed by the above process.
constraints: The given edges form a tree.
1≤Vi≤N1 \le V_i \le N1≤Vi​≤N for each 1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N
Sum of NNN over all test cases will be ≤2000 \le 2000≤2000

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The tree looks like 1↔2↔31\leftrightarrow 2\leftrightarrow 31↔2↔3. The process is as follows:

Initially, B=[V1]=[3]B = [V_1] = [3]B=[V1​]=[3] and S={1}S = \{1\}S={1}.
The only choice of second vertex is x=2x = 2x=2.
This makes S={1,2}S = \{1, 2\}S={1,2} and appends V2=1V_2 = 1V2​=1 to BBB, so we have B=[3,1]B = [3, 1]B=[3,1].
This leaves x=3x=3x=3 as the final vertex.
This makes S={1,2,3}S = \{1, 2, 3\}S={1,2,3} and appends V3=2V_3 = 2V3​=2 to BBB, so we have B=[3,1,2]B = [3, 1, 2]B=[3,1,2].
The answer is hence [3,1,2][3, 1, 2][3,1,2].

Test case 222: The optimal sequence of vertices to choose, after the root, is (2,4,7,3,6,5)(2, 4, 7, 3, 6, 5)(2,4,7,3,6,5).
This gives the sequence B=[1,3,2,1,3,2,4]B = [1, 3, 2, 1, 3, 2, 4]B=[1,3,2,1,3,2,4] which can be proved to be optimal.
